subject: Cardiology
title: Overview of cardiac arrhythmias
preambleMarkdown:
# Overview of cardiac arrhythmias

> **Clinical-review notice:** This is an examination-focused draft. Local protocols, medication choices, procedure thresholds, anticoagulation decisions, pregnancy/pediatric care, and device or surgical indications must be checked against current institutional and national guidance before clinical use.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Overview of cardiac arrhythmias concerns abnormal impulse formation, impulse conduction, or rhythm interpretation. Exam questions focus on hemodynamic stability, ECG pattern recognition, reversible causes, anticoagulation when relevant, and when to use medications, cardioversion, pacing, ablation, or defibrillation.

sections.heading: Key Points
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Unstable rhythm patients need immediate synchronized cardioversion, pacing, or defibrillation depending on rhythm.
- Always correct reversible causes such as hypoxia, ischemia, electrolyte disturbance, medication toxicity, and thyroid disease.
- A 12-lead ECG during symptoms is high value.
- Wide-complex tachycardia should be treated as ventricular tachycardia until proven otherwise.
- Stroke prevention is central in atrial fibrillation and flutter.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Overview of cardiac arrhythmias is a rhythm or conduction disorder diagnosed primarily by ECG or ambulatory rhythm monitoring in the correct clinical context.

sections.heading: Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Supraventricular tachyarrhythmias.
- Ventricular tachyarrhythmias.
- Bradyarrhythmias and conduction block.
- Premature beats and benign variants.
- Inherited or acquired channelopathies.

sections.heading: Epidemiology
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Arrhythmia frequency rises with age, structural heart disease, ischemia, heart failure, valve disease, sleep apnea, thyroid disease, electrolyte disturbance, stimulant exposure, inherited syndromes, and medication effects. Exams focus on who is unstable, who needs anticoagulation, and who needs rhythm-specific emergency treatment.

sections.heading: Anatomy
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Relevant anatomy is the sinus node, atria, AV node, His-Purkinje system, ventricles, accessory pathways, pulmonary vein triggers, and myocardial scar. Lesion location explains PR interval changes, bundle-branch block, reentry circuits, pre-excitation, and ablation targets.

sections.heading: Physiology
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Normal rhythm depends on automaticity, triggered activity control, conduction velocity, refractory periods, autonomic tone, electrolyte gradients, and action-potential duration. Tachyarrhythmias reduce filling time; bradyarrhythmias reduce cardiac output; irregular rhythm can impair atrial contribution to filling.

sections.heading: Pathophysiology
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Arrhythmias arise from abnormal automaticity, triggered activity, reentry, conduction block, accessory pathways, channelopathy, ischemic scar, atrial stretch, electrolyte abnormality, or drug toxicity. Hemodynamic impact depends on rate, ventricular function, AV synchrony, and rhythm duration.

sections.heading: Etiology
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Common triggers include ischemia, hypoxemia, potassium or magnesium disturbance, heart failure, valve disease, myocarditis, thyroid disease, alcohol, stimulants, medications that slow conduction or prolong QT, congenital channelopathy, and postoperative states.

sections.heading: Risk Factors
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Ask about structural heart disease, prior MI, heart failure, congenital heart disease, family sudden death, syncope, QT-prolonging drugs, electrolyte loss, kidney disease, thyroid disease, sleep apnea, alcohol, cocaine, amphetamines, and endurance-athlete history.

sections.heading: Genetics
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Genetic contribution is most important when presentation is early, familial, syndromic, associated with sudden death, or linked to cardiomyopathy, channelopathy, aortopathy, or congenital heart disease. First-degree relatives may need screening when an inherited syndrome is suspected.

sections.heading: Microbiology
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Microbiology is usually not the primary driver unless infection causes endocarditis, myocarditis, pericarditis, infected aneurysm, sepsis-related cardiovascular collapse, prosthetic-device infection, or vascular graft infection. Fever, bacteremia, immunosuppression, injection drug use, or recent procedures should prompt an infectious evaluation.

sections.heading: Clinical Presentation
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Palpitations, lightheadedness, syncope, dyspnea, chest discomfort, fatigue, or sudden collapse.
- Some patients are asymptomatic and diagnosed on ECG or monitoring.
- Hemodynamic compromise suggests rapid rate, bradycardia, structural heart disease, or malignant ventricular rhythm.

sections.heading: History
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Ask about onset, duration, triggers, exertional component, positional or pleuritic features, syncope, dyspnea, palpitations, chest pain, neurologic symptoms, bleeding, fever, medication use, stimulant use, pregnancy, recent procedures, prior ECG or imaging, prior cardiovascular disease, and family history of sudden death or inherited disease.

sections.heading: Physical Examination
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Assess vital signs, oxygenation, mental status, perfusion, volume status, pulses, jugular venous pressure, precordial impulse, heart sounds, murmurs, lungs, edema, neurologic deficits, skin findings, and signs of shock. Compare bilateral pulses and blood pressures when aortic or vascular disease is possible.

sections.heading: Red Flags
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Syncope during exertion.
- Wide-complex tachycardia.
- Pre-excited atrial fibrillation.
- Bradycardia with hypotension or altered mental status.
- Family history of sudden death.
- QT prolongation with torsades risk.

sections.heading: Differential Diagnosis
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Mimics and triggers include acute coronary syndrome, pulmonary embolism, sepsis, anemia, thyrotoxicosis, panic symptoms, hypoglycemia, medication toxicity, dehydration, electrolyte disorders, and vasovagal or orthostatic syncope.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Approach
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Obtain 12-lead ECG and compare with prior tracings.
- Check potassium, magnesium, renal function, medication exposures, thyroid studies when indicated, and ischemia markers when clinically appropriate.
- Use telemetry, Holter, patch monitor, event monitor, or implantable loop recorder based on symptom frequency.
- Use echocardiography when structural disease is suspected.

sections.heading: Investigations
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Use 12-lead ECG, telemetry, electrolytes, renal function, magnesium, troponin when ischemia is possible, thyroid studies when indicated, medication review, ambulatory monitoring based on symptom frequency, echocardiography for structural disease, and electrophysiology testing when diagnosis or ablation planning requires it.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Criteria
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Diagnosis should combine symptoms, examination, ECG or rhythm evidence, imaging, biomarkers, and accepted disease-specific criteria. Avoid diagnosing from one abnormal test in isolation when the clinical syndrome does not fit.

sections.heading: Severity Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Severity is based on hemodynamic stability, symptom burden, organ dysfunction, ECG or imaging risk features, biomarker pattern, ventricular function, lesion severity, arrhythmia risk, embolic risk, bleeding risk, frailty, and need for urgent procedural intervention.

sections.heading: Management
sections.bodyMarkdown:
### Initial Management

- Assess airway, breathing, circulation, disability, and exposure. Place unstable patients on monitoring, obtain IV access, treat hypoxemia, correct immediately reversible causes, obtain an ECG, and escalate early to cardiology, emergency medicine, ICU, vascular surgery, electrophysiology, or cardiac surgery when high-risk features are present.

### Non-pharmacological Management

- Use education, smoking cessation, exercise or cardiac rehabilitation when appropriate, diet quality, weight management, sleep apnea treatment, alcohol or stimulant reduction, vaccination when relevant, and shared decision-making. Activity restrictions depend on acuity, symptoms, rhythm risk, structural disease, and procedure plans.

### Pharmacological Management

- Medication choices depend on the exact syndrome and contraindications. Common classes include antiplatelets, anticoagulants, statins, antihypertensives, beta blockers, calcium-channel blockers, nitrates, diuretics, guideline-directed heart-failure therapies, antiarrhythmics, antibiotics, or analgesics. Dose details require local protocol and patient-specific renal, hepatic, pregnancy, bleeding, and interaction review.

### Procedures

- Procedural options may include cardioversion, defibrillation, temporary or permanent pacing, catheter ablation, coronary angiography or PCI, CABG, valve intervention, vascular repair, thrombectomy, thrombolysis, pericardiocentesis, endovascular repair, or diagnostic catheterization depending on the topic.

### Surgical Management

- Surgery or structural intervention is considered for unstable anatomy, refractory symptoms despite medical therapy, high-risk coronary or valvular disease, congenital lesions, aortic disease, vascular complications, device infection, or complications such as mechanical rupture. Timing depends on acuity, anatomy, procedural risk, expected durability, and patient goals.

- Assess stability first.
- Use vagal maneuvers, adenosine, rate-control agents, antiarrhythmics, cardioversion, pacing, defibrillation, or ablation based on rhythm and patient context.
- Avoid AV-nodal blockers in pre-excited atrial fibrillation.
- Plan long-term management around symptoms, structural disease, recurrence risk, and anticoagulation indications.

sections.heading: Monitoring
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Monitor symptoms, vital signs, rhythm, perfusion, urine output when ill, medication adverse effects, renal function and electrolytes when using diuretics or renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system therapy, anticoagulation safety, device function when present, and disease-specific imaging intervals.

sections.heading: Follow-up
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Follow-up should confirm diagnosis, review response to therapy, reconcile medications, address risk factors, arrange rehabilitation or specialist care, document warning symptoms, and ensure surveillance for recurrence, progression, procedural complications, or adverse drug effects.

sections.heading: Complications
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Complications may include shock, heart failure, pulmonary edema, myocardial infarction, stroke, systemic embolism, sudden cardiac death, malignant arrhythmia, bleeding from therapy, renal injury, infection, recurrent hospitalization, procedure-related injury, and chronic functional limitation.

sections.heading: Prognosis
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Prognosis depends on acuity, ventricular function, comorbid disease, age, kidney function, adherence, response to therapy, reversibility of the cause, and access to timely emergency or procedural care. Early recognition and appropriate follow-up reduce preventable morbidity.

sections.heading: Prevention
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Prevention includes smoking cessation, blood-pressure control, lipid management, diabetes care, physical activity, diet quality, weight management, treatment of sleep apnea, avoidance of cardiotoxic exposures, vaccination when relevant, and family screening for inherited disease when indicated.

sections.heading: Patient Education
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Explain the diagnosis, uncertainty that remains, warning symptoms, medication purpose and adverse effects, follow-up plan, activity guidance, and when to seek urgent care. Use plain language and confirm understanding.

sections.heading: Nursing Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Trend vital signs, oxygenation, pain, mental status, perfusion, rhythm, intake/output when ill, and response to treatment.
- Escalate promptly for red flags, new neurologic symptoms, recurrent chest pain, hypotension, syncope, worsening dyspnea, or malignant rhythm.
- Reinforce medication adherence, follow-up, lifestyle changes, and procedure instructions.

sections.heading: Pharmacy Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Check allergies, renal and hepatic function, pregnancy status when relevant, interactions, duplicate anticoagulant or antiplatelet therapy, QT-prolonging drugs, and adherence barriers.
- High-risk medications require indication, duration, monitoring plan, and bleeding or toxicity counseling.
- Reconcile medications at transitions of care.

sections.heading: Dentistry Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Most cardiology conditions do not automatically require antibiotic prophylaxis for dental care. Consider endocarditis prophylaxis only for guideline-defined high-risk cardiac conditions and procedures, and coordinate anticoagulant management for invasive dental procedures when needed.

sections.heading: Special Populations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
### Pediatrics

- Pediatric presentations may reflect congenital disease, inherited arrhythmia, myocarditis, Kawasaki disease, multisystem inflammatory syndromes, or rare familial vascular disease. Use age-specific vital signs, doses, and pediatric specialist pathways.

### Pregnancy

- Pregnancy changes heart rate, plasma volume, coagulation, and imaging or medication choices. Maternal stabilization comes first in emergencies. Coordinate cardiology, obstetrics, anesthesia, and neonatology for significant disease.

### Older Adults

- Older adults often have atypical symptoms, polypharmacy, frailty, kidney disease, cognitive impairment, and higher bleeding or procedural risk. Goals of care and functional status should shape decisions.

### Renal Impairment

- Renal disease changes biomarker interpretation, medication dosing, contrast risk, electrolyte vulnerability, and bleeding risk. Avoid delaying life-saving imaging or intervention when the emergency risk is greater than contrast risk.

### Hepatic Impairment

- Assess coagulopathy, thrombocytopenia, drug metabolism, volume status, and procedural bleeding risk. Medication selection and anticoagulation decisions require individualized review.

### Immunocompromised Patients

- Maintain a lower threshold for infection, atypical presentations, drug interactions, and device-related complications. Coordinate antimicrobial and procedural decisions with specialists when infection is possible.

sections.heading: Guideline Comparison
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Major North American and European guidance generally agrees on stabilizing emergencies first, using ECG and echocardiography appropriately, treating modifiable risk factors aggressively, and matching procedures to anatomy and risk. Differences commonly involve thresholds, timing, scoring systems, medication preferences, and local resource pathways. Use current local guidance for patient care.

sections.heading: Clinical Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Stability determines the first action.
- Compare current ECG, imaging, and symptoms with prior data whenever possible.
- Treat the patient, not a single number.
- Reassess after each intervention.
- Imported notes below are provenance; current synthesis should be reviewed against up-to-date guidelines.

sections.heading: Common Mistakes
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Delaying emergency treatment for nonessential testing.
- Ignoring medication contraindications, renal function, pregnancy, or bleeding risk.
- Assuming a normal initial test excludes a dynamic cardiovascular process.
- Failing to arrange follow-up after an ED or inpatient diagnosis.
- Removing imported source notes instead of preserving provenance.

sections.heading: Exam Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
### USMLE Step 1

- Focus on anatomy, physiology, pathology, pharmacology, ECG mechanisms, hemodynamics, and classic disease associations.

### USMLE Step 2 CK

- Prioritize presentation, next best diagnostic test, initial stabilization, red flags, and first-line management.

### USMLE Step 3

- Emphasize sequencing: stabilize first, choose the right unit of care, monitor treatment response, prevent complications, and plan discharge follow-up.

### ABIM

- Expect guideline nuance, comorbid disease, renal impairment, anticoagulation decisions, procedural indications, and treatment failure.

### ABFM

- Focus on prevention, screening when applicable, risk-factor control, medication adherence, counseling, and recognizing emergencies that need transfer.

### Surgery boards

- Know when vascular, cardiac, or structural intervention is urgent; understand anatomy, perioperative risk, and complications.

sections.heading: High-Yield Facts
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Unstable tachycardia: synchronized cardioversion unless pulseless.
- Pulseless VT/VF: defibrillation and cardiac arrest algorithm.
- Symptomatic high-grade AV block often needs pacing.
- Correct potassium and magnesium in ventricular arrhythmias.
- AF stroke prevention depends on thromboembolic risk, not just rate.

sections.heading: Evidence Gaps
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Areas requiring ongoing review include individualized thresholds for intervention, risk prediction in underrepresented groups, long-term comparative effectiveness of procedures and devices, optimal antithrombotic duration, and implementation of guideline-directed therapy in patients with multimorbidity.

sections.heading: References
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- ACC/AHA/ACEP/NAEMSP/SCAI. 2025 Guideline for the Management of Patients With Acute Coronary Syndromes. https://professional.heart.org/en/science-news/2025-guideline-for-the-management-of-patients-with-acute-coronary-syndromes
- AHA/ACC/HFSA. 2022 Guideline for the Management of Heart Failure. https://professional.heart.org/en/science-news/2022-guideline-for-the-management-of-heart-failure
- ACC/AHA/ACCP/HRS. 2023 Guideline for the Diagnosis and Management of Atrial Fibrillation. https://www.ahajournals.org/doi/10.1161/CIR.0000000000001193
- ACC/AHA. 2020 Guideline for the Management of Patients With Valvular Heart Disease. https://professional.heart.org/en/science-news/2020-acc-aha-guideline-for-the-management-of-patients-with-valvular-heart-disease
- ACC/AHA. 2022 Guideline for the Diagnosis and Management of Aortic Disease. https://professional.heart.org/en/science-news/2022-guideline-for-the-diagnosis-and-management-of-aortic-disease
- ACC/AHA. 2024 Guideline for the Management of Lower Extremity Peripheral Artery Disease. https://professional.heart.org/-/media/PHD-Files-2/Science-News/2/2024/2024-PAD-guideline-slide-set.pdf

### Source Excerpt: Rhythm Disturbances

_Source lines: 3419-4101._

```text
IV. RHYTHM DISTURBANCES
Rhythm disturbances can be classified as belonging to one of three major groups:
supraventricular dysrhythmias, ventricular dysrhythmias, and disorders of cardiac
conduction. Table 2-4 contains a summary of the drugs often used to manage dys-
rhythmias in the ED.

A. Supraventricular dysrhythmias arise in or above the AV node.

1. Sinus arrhythmia occurs in young, healthy patients and refers to the cyclic
variations of heart rate induced by respiration. Heart rate increases with inspi-
ration and declines with expiration.

2. Sinus tachycardia is a response to many pathologic processes, including fever,
hypovolemia, hemorrhage, pain, hypoxia, and many drugs. It can also be a
normal response to exertion or emotion.

a. ECG findings include an atrial rate of greater than 100/min (even higher in in-
fants and children) with a 1:1 ratio between atrial and ventricular contraction.

b. Therapy consists of identification and treatment of the cause.

3. Multifocal atrial tachycardia and wandering atrial pacemaker. Multifocal
atrial tachycardia is usually seen in association with chronic lung disease. Wan-
dering atrial pacemaker is seen in very young patients and in athletes.

a. ECG findings include P waves of three or more different morphologies,
with different PR intervals. Multifocal atrial tachycardia is characterized by
an atrial rate of greater than 100/min, whereas wandering atrial pacemaker
is characterized by an atrial rate of less than 100/min.

b. Therapy is directed toward improvement of pulmonary function. If a high heart
rate is contributing to the patient's distress and improvement of pulmonary
function fails to reduce it, intravenous magnesium and diltiazem may be tried.

4. Atrial flutter is seen in association with disorders that cause myocardial injury
or inflammation (e.g., ischemic heart disease, MI) and pulmonary embolism.

Specifically, this is a reentry tachydysrhythmia associated within the portal-
caval isthmus.

a. ECG findings include an atrial rate of 250 to 350/min. Classically, a ventric-
ular rate of 150/min with a 2:1 AV block is seen (Table 2-5 and Figure 2-5),
although 3:1 and 4:1 AV blocks are possible. Patients with accessory bypass
tracts may have ventricular rates high enough to place the patient at serious
risk of ventricular fibrillation (see IV.B.4).

b. Therapy
i. Pharmacologic therapy. Diltiazem, verapamil, digoxin, # blockers, and
procainamide can all convert the patient to sinus rhythm by slowing AV
conduction (and therefore, the ventricular rate).

ii. Direct current synchronized cardioversion at 25 to 50 joules converts
90% of patients to sinus rhythm and is indicated for unstable patients.

5. Atrial fibrillation. Several medical conditions are often associated with atrial
fibrillation, especially cardiac ischemia, pulmonary embolism, hypertension,
COPD, alcohol intoxication, mitral valve disease (often in patients with a his-
tory of rheumatic heart disease), pericarditis, and thyrotoxicosis.

a. Clinical findings. The pulse is irregularly irregular. CHF can occur either
as a result of a precipitating ischemic event or as a result of acute loss of the
atrial contribution to ventricular filling (i.e., the "atrial kick").

b. ECG findings include an irregularly irregular ventricular rhythm with
fine fibrillatory waves that is seen best in the right chest leads. Ventricular
rates are typically around 160/min. In patients who are not taking AV node
blockers (e.g., digoxin), a slow ventricular response to acute atrial fibrilla-
tion indicates extensive heart disease.

c. Therapy
i. Hemodynamically unstable patients are cardioverted first with 100
joules and then with 200 joules.

ii. In hemodynamically stable patients, ventricular rate control is obtained
using intravenous diltiazem and # blockers. Chemical cardioversion
with procainamide can then be effected, barring any contraindications.

A
Rhythm strip from a patient with atrial flutter with 2:1 conduction. At point A, adenosine
(12 mg) was administered, resulting in complete atrioventricular block and revealing flutter
waves at a rate of 300/min.

2-5
F I G U R E
iii. A patient with symptoms suggestive of atrial fibrillation that last for more
than 2 days is at risk of intracardiac thrombus formation and systemic em-
bolization with cardioversion. These patients need ventricular rate control
and systemic anticoagulation therapy for 2 weeks prior to cardioversion.

6. Supraventricular tachycardia
a. Wide-complex supraventricular tachycardia cannot reliably be distin-
guished from ventricular tachycardia, and is therefore treated as ventricular
tachycardia (see IV.B).

b. Narrow-complex supraventricular tachycardia
i. Reentrant supraventricular tachycardia
a) Cause. In the majority of patients with supraventricular tachycar-
dia, the dysrhythmia is related to the mechanism of reentry. An
abnormal loop of conductive tissue in the AV node or a bypass tract
allows a circus rhythm to develop, with ventricular depolarization
occurring at a rate of 140 to 200/min. Reentrant supraventricular
tachycardia can occur in patients with normal hearts, and also in as-
sociation with MI, pericarditis, mitral valve prolapse, or one of the
preexcitation syndromes (see IV.C.5). The onset is usually sudden.

b) ECG findings. Inverted, retrograde-conducted P waves are often
buried in the QRS complex or found in the ST segment (Figure 2-6).

c) Therapy
1) Unstable patients. Direct current synchronized cardioversion
should be carried out, starting with 50 joules.

F I G U R E
C A R D I O V A S C U L A R E M E R G E N C I E S 	 	45

Cardiovascular Emergencies
2) Stable patients. Vagal maneuvers (e.g., facial immersion in cold
water, the Valsalva maneuver, carotid massage) can be attempted.

Carotid massage is performed by applying steady pressure on the
carotid for 10 seconds after first palpating for bilaterally intact
carotid pulsations and auscultating for carotid bruits. If neither
of these is present, the carotid on the side of the nondominant
hemisphere should be massaged.

Adenosine, diltiazem, verapamil, digoxin, or ! blocker therapy
can be tried if vagal maneuvers are unsuccessful. If adenosine is
used, it is essential that it be administered rapidly through a large
vein (antecubital or more proximal), followed by a saline flush.

ii. Ectopic supraventricular tachycardia
a) Cause. Ectopic supraventricular tachycardia is often caused by di-
goxin toxicity; in this situation, it is often associated with AV block.

Ectopic supraventricular tachycardia also occurs with myocardial
ischemia, lung disease, and alcohol abuse. The onset and resolution
are usually gradual.

b) ECG findings. Ectopic supraventricular tachycardia is characterized
on the ECG by P waves before each QRS complex.

c) Therapy
1) If digoxin toxicity is suspected, treatment entails discontinua-
tion of the drug and a stat drug level. If the patient is unstable,
cardioversion is contraindicated, and digoxin-specific Fab may
need to be given, in consultation with a toxicologist. Magnesium
and phenytoin can also be used.

2) In patients who have not been taking digoxin, treatment should
be directed toward the underlying cause. Agents that decrease au-
tomaticity, AV conduction, or both (e.g., procainamide, # blockers,
calcium channel blockers, digoxin) may also be administered.

7. Sinus bradycardia
a. Causes. Sinus bradycardia occurs in three situations:
i. As a normal finding in athletes
ii. Secondary to drug therapy with sympatholytics, AV blockers, or vago-
tonics (e.g., # blockers, digoxin, calcium channel blockers, narcotics,
miotic glaucoma medications)
iii. Secondary to pathologic processes, especially myocardial ischemia, but
also hypothermia, hypothyroidism, and increased intracranial pressure
b. ECG findings include normal-width QRS complexes preceded by P waves
at a rate less than 60/min.

c. Therapy consists of atropine for hemodynamically unstable patients fol-
lowed by elective pacemaker placement, depending on the clinical circum-
stances. Patients with signs of hypoperfusion may need immediate external
or internal pacing if atropine is ineffective. Epinephrine and dopamine can
be used as adjunctive therapy if there is difficulty achieving pacemaker cap-
ture or if the hypotension persists.

8. Junctional arrhythmias arise at the AV node and occur in situations of impaired
sinus node discharge, impaired AV node conduction, and increased nodal auto-
maticity. All of these mechanisms are associated with digoxin toxicity and dis-
eases of the myocardium, especially ischemia, cardiomyopathy, and myocarditis.

a. Premature junctional beats usually occur in patients with myocardial isch-
emia or in those with digoxin toxicity. The ECG reveals normal-appearing
QRS complexes that occur prematurely, with no antecedent P wave. Treat-
ment is of the underlying disease, or with procainamide.

b. Junctional rhythm is characterized by a regular narrow-complex QRS mor-
phology, at a rate of about 40 to 60 beats/min. Usually there is no evidence
of atrial activity, which is the reason the junctional pacemaker cells have
taken control. If P waves are seen, unrelated to the QRS complexes, the pa-
tient is in third-degree AV block (see IV.C.3; Figure 2-7).

46 	 	S T E P - U P T O E M E R G E N C Y M E D I C I N E

Cardiovascular Emergencies
c. Accelerated junctional rhythms are characterized by normal-width QRS
complexes at a rate of 60 to 100 beats/min and an absence of P waves.

d. Junctional tachycardia is characterized by a rate greater than 100 beats/min.

B. Ventricular dysrhythmias arise below the AV node.

1. Premature ventricular contractions (PVCs) are often observed in people with
healthy hearts. However, they are more common in cardiac disease, especially
ischemic states. They are also seen in conditions of increased myocardial
irritability, such as severe electrolyte or acid-base imbalances or toxicity from
stimulants or digoxin.

a. ECG findings include premature, wide, bizarre-appearing QRS complexes,
not preceded by a P wave. The QRS vector is usually opposite that of nor-
mally conducted complexes. There is typically a full compensatory pause
because the SA node is not reset by the PVC.

b. Therapy. In the setting of acute MI, PVCs should prompt a search for recur-
rent ischemia or metabolic abnormalities that need to be treated.

2. Ventricular (wide-complex) tachycardia usually occurs in patients with seri-
ous heart disease. Other causes include severe electrolyte and acid-base dis-
orders and toxicity from drugs, especially stimulant drugs of abuse, tricyclic
antidepressants, and digoxin.

a. ECG findings. Even experienced cardiologists cannot reliably distinguish
supraventricular tachycardia with aberrant conduction (wide-complex supra-
ventricular tachycardia) from ventricular tachycardia on the basis of the ECG.

i. The ECG reveals a wide QRS complex (greater than 0.12 seconds, or
3 mm). The rhythm is usually regular (there may be beat-to-beat varia-
tion) and ranges from 100 to 200 beats/min. P waves independent of
ventricular complexes (AV dissociation) may be noted, confirming the
presence of ventricular tachycardia (Figure 2-8).

ii. Torsades de pointes are runs of ventricular tachycardia, usually lasting
20 to 30 seconds, in which the amplitude of the QRS complexes waxes
and wanes in any given lead. Torsades is associated with any condition
that causes prolongation of cardiac repolarization, such as hypokalemia,
bradycardia, or toxicity from antiarrhythmic agents.

Quick 	HIT

PVCs occur in most patients
and are not predictive of
which patients will develop
ventricular fibrillation.

sustained ventricular tachy-
cardia with antiarrhythmic
agents is not advocated by
authorities in cardiology.

Cardiovascular Emergencies
b. Therapy
i. Unstable patients should undergo direct current cardioversion, starting
at 50 joules. Pulseless ventricular tachycardia is treated like ventricular
fibrillation, starting with 200 joules (see IV.B.4).

ii. Stable patients are treated with amiodarone or procainamide. If the
wide-complex tachycardia is suspected to be supraventricular tachycar-
dia with abnormal conduction, adenosine can be used for both diagnos-
tic and therapeutic effect.

iii. Torsades de pointes is treated with intravenous magnesium sulfate.

Type Ic and type Ia antiarrhythmic agents are contraindicated.

3. Accelerated idioventricular tachycardia is seen in association with acute MI.

The QRS morphology is similar to that of ventricular tachycardia, but the rate
is 40 to 100 beats/min. Accelerated idioventricular tachycardia should not be
treated with lidocaine because it may represent the only functioning pacemaker
in the heart.

4. Ventricular fibrillation is caused by chaotically disorganized electrical activity.

Because there is no concerted electrical activity, there is no cardiac contraction
and no perfusion.

a. ECG findings. The ECG shows an irregular waveform of varying amplitude
(see Figure 2-6).

b. Therapy. The treatment is defibrillation, cardiopulmonary resuscitation,
epinephrine, lidocaine, and procainamide, as per current Advanced Cardiac
Life Support protocols.

C. Disorders of cardiac conduction are traditionally divided into those occurring in
the AV node ("AV blocks") and those occurring in one or more of the main con-
duction fascicles of the His-Purkinje system ("bundle branch blocks"). However,
AV blocks can occur in the bundle of His and the proximal Purkinje system as
well as in the AV node.

1. First-degree AV block is really prolonged AV conduction and is usually found
in patients with cardiac ischemia, rheumatic fever, myocarditis, or toxicity from
digoxin or other AV blockers. First-degree AV block is diagnosed by finding a
prolonged PR interval (i.e., greater than 0.20 seconds) on the ECG.

2. Second-degree (intermittent) AV block. Only some P waves are transmitted.

Traditionally, second-degree AV block is classified as type I or type II; however,
pathologically, the important distinction to make is between those conduction
disturbances that are high in the ventricular conduction system and those that
are low in the conduction system. The former, "low-grade" blocks, are less likely
than the latter, "high-grade" blocks, to progress to complete heart block, and if
they do, the rescue pacemaker is usually high in the conduction system, allowing
for coordinated ventricular depolarization at a physiologically acceptable rate.

a. Mobitz type I (Wenckebach) block. The same conditions that can cause
first-degree AV block can cause a Mobitz type I block.

i. ECG findings. The PR intervals become progressively longer until a P
wave is completely blocked.

ii. Therapy. If the patient is asymptomatic, no treatment is required. Symp-
tomatic patients are treated as for bradycardia (see IV.A.7.c).

b. Mobitz type II block is also associated with the same clinical conditions as
first-degree AV block.

i. ECG findings. Mobitz type II block is characterized by nonconducted P
waves that are not heralded by progressive PR prolongation. It is often
caused by disease of the distal bundle of His or proximal Purkinje sys-
tem, so that there might be ECG evidence of concomitant fascicular or
bundle branch blocks.

ii. Therapy. For patients without antecedent MI, Mobitz type II block is
treated as discussed in IV.A.7.c. Most patients with MI require prophy-
lactic pacemaker placement.

3. Third-degree AV block (complete heart block) is characterized by AV dissocia-
tion with no relation between P waves and QRS complexes (see Figure 2-5).

The causes and treatment are similar to those of Mobitz type II block.

can be the last effort of an

48 	 	S T E P - U P T O E M E R G E N C Y M E D I C I N E

Cardiovascular Emergencies
4. Fascicular and bundle branch blocks can be a sign of many forms of myocar-
dial pathology but are most significant when they develop in association with
acute MI. In patients with acute MI, the development of fascicular or bundle
branch blocks signals extensive myocardial damage, the potential for unstable
heart blocks, and the possible need for pacemaker placement.

a. Left anterior hemiblock is identified by the presence of a normal-width
QRS complex and a leftward QRS axis of less than & 45 degrees.

b. Left posterior hemiblock is suggested by a normal-width QRS complex and
a rightward QRS axis of greater than 110 degrees.

c. Right bundle branch block is diagnosed by a prolonged QRS complex
(i.e., greater than 0.12 seconds) and a triphasic QRS complex in lead V1-
the RSR' pattern-and a prominent S wave in V6. Right bundle branch block
is seen in ischemic and valvular heart disease and may be idiopathic.

d. Left bundle branch block causes a QRS complex of a duration greater than
0.12 seconds with wide, predominantly negative complexes in lead V1 and
positive complexes in V6 . Left bundle branch block is most commonly as-
sociated with ischemia, longstanding hypertension, cardiomyopathy, and
severe aortic valve disease.

e. Bifascicular blocks are identified by the presence of either left anterior or
left posterior hemiblocks with right bundle branch block, or left bundle
branch block alone.

5. Preexcitation syndromes are caused by abnormal bypass tracts between the
atrial and ventricular conduction systems. These fibers can generate dysrhyth-
mias by providing routes for the circus rhythms of reentrant tachycardias or by
permitting abnormal retrograde conduction of ventricular impulses to the atria.

Wolff-Parkinson-White (WPW) syndrome is the most common preexcitation
syndrome; it is characterized by a high incidence of tachydysrhythmias-
approximately 75% supraventricular tachycardias and 25% atrial fibrillation.

Patients with WPW and atrial fibrillation may present with a rapid wide-
complex ventricular response that can deteriorate to ventricular fibrillation
if treated with AV nodal blockers (e.g., digoxin, calcium channel blockers,

# blockers). Treatment is with intravenous procainamide.
```
